text: Haiyan County was originally Wu Yuan County, which belonged to the Kuaiji commandery during the Qin dynasty and continued to do so during the Han dynasty. According to the *Geography Records*, there was a canal official in the old Wu Yuan. The *Huan Yu Ji* states that the salt fields were widely spread and could be seen from afar. The *Han Geography* mentions that the county had a salt official. Five miles east is Wu Yuan, which Wang Mang changed to Zhan Wu County during the Xin dynasty. Later, it became a lake (the Tu Lake), and the administrative center was moved to Wu Yuan village, changing the name to Wu Yuan County again. It was later changed to Haiyan County. In the second year of Emperor An of Han, it became a lake again (the Dang Lake), and the old city was moved to become the county of Guoyi. It was then moved to the sea dike and named Hai Tang County. In the second year of Jingyun of Tang, it belonged to Suzhou. It was abolished in the second year of Tianxian and re-established in the fifth year of Kaiyuan. In the fourth year of Tianfu of Jin, the state of Xiu was established, and it belonged to it. The county had 18 and 19 districts of Wu Yuan. However, the 18 district was divided into upper and lower villages. One is at the 18-li weir, bordering Qi Jing; one is at the lake, from the lakehead to the boundary bridge, bordering Jiaxing. Based on the base of De Cang temple and the records of Daoist institutions, the 17 district has its own Da Yi, and the 17 district should not have two. Since the geographical connections are different, it is unknown what name. Now only the upper and lower 18 districts are considered definite.

The Lake When It Was First Formed The Lake When It Was First Formed is located fifty li north of the current county, with a length of twelve li from north to south and six li from east to west. According to ancient traditions passed down through generations, when the land first began to sink, a woman gave birth to a creature that resembled a dragon or sea monster. When the creature was washed into the water, the land sank, and it moved northeast. Today, there is the Maogang River, which directly connects to Lake Tai. Recently, a stone inscription was discovered, which is the tombstone of Lord Lu of Wu Prefecture during the Tang Dynasty, buried in Qijing Township, Haisalt County, Suzhou, at the Lake When It Was First Formed. Thus, the name of the lake is ancient. Some say that the *Parrot Island Gazetteer* does not mention this lake, suggesting that it might have existed before the land sank. In the past, a fisherman found a long iron chain in the lake, so long that it nearly capsized his boat, and he discarded it out of fear. Some say that the chain was attached to the creature. From the Han Dynasty to the present, over a thousand years have passed, and the lake has gradually become shallower while the land has gradually increased in size. Occasionally, people have reportedly seen traces of the creature's remains in the lake.

Mount Jinshan's Loyal and Valiant King, the Han Bo Lu Hou (博陆侯) of the Huo (霍) clan, was said to have been born during the reign of Sun Quan of Wu. One day, a low-ranking eunuch or servant reported: "The sovereign's domain extends to Huating Valley, far southwest of which lies Mount Jinshan and its saline pond. The lake poses a threat to the people, who catch fish and turtles from it for food, but human efforts alone cannot prevent its harmful effects. Once, Mount Jinshan was part of Haiyan County. At some point, the area sank and became a lake, without the protection of a great deity. I am the loyal servant and hero Huo (某), with powerful allies, capable of controlling the area. A temple should be built on the mountain. The king of Wu then established a temple, and during the Jianyan period, a palace was built in Danghu, which was granted a name. The king's influence was particularly evident, and local people's prayers were answered. His subordinate, Lord Qian, was especially revered. The king's birthday is celebrated on April 18th, and from the eastern and western parts of Zhejiang, merchants and their boats come in large numbers to pay homage. From early April to mid-month, the market is bustling and lively. People set up altars among pine and cypress trees, worship their ancestors, and cry before returning. This is known as the Small Mount Yue Temple, which houses four bronze statues of saints brought from the sea, still present today.

During the reign of Emperor Gaozong, Fang La rebelled, causing great alarm in the region west of the Zhejiang River. At that time, the Lu family lived in Dongwu, still poor and insignificant. The fleeing soldiers who remained in Suzhou and Xiushui scattered and ran in all directions, causing great distress among the villagers. The Lu family worshiped Zhenwu, the True Warrior, and found their prayers to be extremely effective. Due to their prayers, they planned to move far away to avoid danger, but the god did not permit it. Shortly after, the fleeing soldiers were about to reach the border when suddenly a black flag appeared in the sky, and a dense fog enveloped everything. The soldiers were greatly frightened and retreated, sparing the border. The Lu family then built a Daoist temple in the west of the city and worshiped it, and it has been effective in answering prayers ever since. The Five Manifest Gods, also known as the Buddha of Splendor according to Buddhist texts, have been active in Wuyuan for a long time, with annual offerings continuing without interruption. During the reign of Emperor Lizong, the villagers were troubled by the long journey to pay homage to the gods, so they sculpted an image of the Buddha of Splendor and enshrined it in the eastern wing of the Dazang Temple, building a pavilion for it. There was a well in front of the shrine, but nobody used to draw water from it. After the shrine was built, people began to draw water from it to drink, and their illnesses were cured. Thereafter, those who drew water and those who prayed did so every day without fail. The monks took advantage of this, requiring anyone who wanted to draw water to pray to the god. If they got a favorable divination, then they could draw water, and they also added a small yellow flag to the well. Thereafter, fewer people came to pray, and now it no longer works. According to old legends, when the lake first flooded, the White Water Lord could not escape on horseback and stopped his horse, pointing his whip to find a spot in the southeast corner of the lake that was not submerged, and built a temple there. Up to now, this place remains the highest. It is also said that there are three brothers, one in Shazao and one in Zhapu, all of whom are called the White Water Temple.

**Translation:** The Temple of De Zang, originally named Baoxing Temple, was established in the first year of the Huichang era of the Tang dynasty (841 AD) but was initially abandoned during the Guangqi era. In the middle period of the Qingtai era of the Later Tang dynasty (934-936 AD), a local villager named Qiu Tai rebuilt it on the old site. During the Dazhong Xiangfu era of the Song dynasty (1008-1016 AD), it was renamed to its current name. At one point, it was changed from a temple to a palace, and then restored as a temple during the Jianyan era of the Song dynasty (1127-1130 AD). The temple has two pavilions and a water and land altar, which was built by a wealthy man named Shen Zhong in previous years. When the altar was set up, if the donor was sincere, the tea would show auspicious flowers, and thus the flowers could be clearly seen, also a rare phenomenon. The Donglin Shuishui Temple was originally the White Lotus Hermitage of the layman Ding'an. The temple has a treasure, which has become very dilapidated over time. The abbot Zhi Xiang gathered the community to restore it, and it was only partially restored, with little inside. Originally, the temple had a revolving treasure, regardless of its size, monks would eat one piece of it, and thus many people came to visit. If someone was sick or possessed, they would be cured by turning the Donglin treasure. The temple has a deity named Shi, enfeoffed as Protector of the Nation, who performs rituals for it, and the monks thus benefited. Forty li north of Qijiang County, there is a temple. The temple is called the Qijiang Gong Temple, or sometimes the Unenlightened Great King. According to ancient legends, Duke Qijiang followed the coast south, observed at Zhuanfu and Chaowu, and once visited here, building a temple. There used to be a stele, but it has now worn away and is no longer there. In the fourteenth year of the Zhenyuan era of the Tang dynasty (798 AD), the epitaph of Lady Zhou, wife of Lord Lu of Wu, states that she was buried in Xingzhi County, to the east of Haiyan County, in the Qijiang Township of Qingdun Original, northwest of the burial ground. Thus, the name Qijiang Township of Qingdun Original is old.

At the end of the Yuanfeng era, after the frost, ice naturally formed flowers on the tiles of houses in Xiu Prefecture. Each tile had one branch, just as if painted by an artist. The branches had large flowers resembling peony leaves, and small flowers resembling daylilies and crabapples, all with leaves and branches, not a single hair missing, even a skilled artist could not replicate them. When copied onto paper, they were no different from stone carvings. The Bao Sheng Stone Buddha Temple is located in the southeast of Jiaxing County. In the second year of the Zhude era of the Tang Dynasty, four stone Buddha statues were excavated from the temple site, and they still exist today. In the Tian圣 era, the temple was granted the name Bao Sheng, but people commonly referred to it as the Stone Buddha Temple (Bao is sometimes written as Bao). In the summer drought of the Gengzi year, the lake area could be traversed by vehicles. A fishing boat docked at the lakeside at night and saw a distant light shining. People thought it must be a treasure buried underground, so they dug it up at midnight and found a well of bricks. Each brick was six or seven inches long, with square holes at each end that fit together, and palm-like patterns on both sides, extremely fine and clearly visible. It is unknown when these bricks first appeared. I suspect they were made by potters of the time. Children were selling them in the market, and some used them as inkstones, which were clear, smooth, delicate, and lovely. I once obtained a brick, which was taken by someone interested in it.

The Ancestral Tomb in Nanlin, the grave of the great-grandfather Xuanxi. I once heard from my uncles and great-uncles that when the site was first being laid out, the great-grandfather had a good understanding of geography and planned to dig a pond and divert water to the southwest of the tomb. One night, he dreamed of a woman who said, "My tomb is in the area where you plan to dig the pond. Please warn the laborers not to damage my tomb, and I will reward you greatly." The next day, several pieces of gold were indeed found at that place, and thus it was used to build a temple. Until today, her tomb still exists. Since its construction, it has been over a hundred years, and the forests and bamboo groves there are more abundant than in other places. In the summer of the year Bǐng Wǔ (丙午), several pairs of bamboo shoots suddenly sprouted, and no one did not marvel. Each had poems and songs written about it. However, they eventually did not grow into full bamboo poles, and there was no other response. Could it be that things deviating from the norm became omens, ultimately withering and dying, so it is said. The tomb of the Right Minister of Guangyan Temple is located near the lake, occupying a strategic position, and is the best in the region. The southeastern side is all lake, with distant peaks arranged like a pen stand, and a tower stands tall in the middle of the waves. Literary talent stands out prominently, and throughout generations, there have been those who attained official positions, more prosperous than other clans. During the year Chongyue (淳祐), suddenly a wisp of smoke appeared between the trees, and near and far, no one did not feel surprised. Those who looked closely saw countless mosquitoes and gnats coming out from the trees, continuously throughout the day. It is said that this smoke was formed by these insects, and no one knows what it signifies.

In the middle of the lake, there is a piece of land where a pagoda was built to adjust the fengshui, and people call it Anshan. The lake is vast and expansive, and thieves often hide in this area. As a result, the temple caretakers dare not stay, and the place has become desolate and overgrown. There is a large hole in it like a jar, extremely hollow below, with a giant python lurking inside. Sometimes, people see it lying on its side beside the lake sand. In recent years, several Taoist monks have lived there, and the Buddha hall and corridors have gradually taken shape, and the snake is no longer seen. My family's house used to have a snake hole in the wall, and every spring, small snakes often appeared, but in recent years, they have become less frequent. Another clan member once had a servant hoeing the grass when suddenly a sound was heard from the ground. Upon digging into the soil about a foot, a stone slab was found covering it very firmly. When it was opened, a jar was found that could store several shi of rice. Inside were eight or nine giant pythons, which ran out in all directions. They were quickly attacked, and some died or fled, but no one knew how they had entered. It is likely that there must be some mysterious creature, possibly one that changed form upon not being encountered. Chen Mountain is located forty li northeast of the county, with a height of eighty-one zhang and a circumference of fifteen li. It has the White Dragon Spring, known for its miraculous healing properties, and the Dragon King Temple, known for its widespread influence. At the top of the mountain is a dragon's lair, which is not very deep, and it does not dry up in spring and summer. When the people encounter drought, they pray at the cave, and there will definitely be a mysterious creature seen. They take the water from it for worship, and rain falls abundantly. There is also the tomb of the Dragon Mother there, and every year, there are often storms in July. People say it is the dragon washing the tomb.

The Temple of the Dragon King on Chen Mountain has a Hall of Guanyin behind it. In a past year, two stones suddenly fell from the middle of a mountain, one rolling into the Guanyin statue from the back wall of the hall, and the other falling to the west of the hall. The roof tiles were undamaged, but it is unclear how the stones entered the hall. Today, the two stones still exist and are also remarkable. Many people left inscriptions. In the year of the Rabbit, I visited the temple and composed a poem on the wall to record the event. Seventy miles north of Hua Ting County lies Dian Lake, with the Temple of the Three Graces on the mountain. Every year, the dragons in the lake compete and fight, causing the water to churn, but they do not enter the temple. The deity is extremely divine and mysterious. The monks relied on the deity's power to provide meals, and the deity is particularly responsive on water and land. In a past year, a fishing boat moored at the lake's mouth. Suddenly, a woman boarded the boat and said she wanted to go to the Dian Mountain Temple. When they reached the shore, the woman went straight into the temple, leaving behind only one shoe. The fisherman took the shoe and went to ask for passage money. The temple monks were very surprised and said, "This must be the manifestation of the Three Graces." So they followed her to the hall and indeed saw her left foot without a shoe, with a hundred coins beside her. Then they gave the fisherman the money and left. The "Jiahe Baiyong" collection says: "The deity resides and protects the yin and yang, searches for and defends against great waves. Do not worry about the anger of dragons; in recent years, they fear the rebuke."

The bell in front of De Cang Temple is cast from bronze, its sound is extremely loud and resonant. I once heard an elderly person say: At the time of casting the bell, a craftsman said that this bell should not be struck immediately; wait until I reach sixty li before striking it. After he had left, the monks at the new temple eighteen li away struck it hastily. The craftsman, hearing the sound, sighed and said: "The sound stops here." Now, the bell in the temple cannot be heard beyond eighteen li from the new temple. Strange indeed! In the wine cellar of Danghu, there is a temple of the Four Saints built after the cooking and washing process, and the temple has been standing for many years. The previous and current officials have taken turns to worship and serve it with great care. Every time a new official arrives, they raise two banners; when they leave office, they lower two banners. As a fixed rule to repay the protection of the gods. Between the years of Bingchen and DingSi, there was a person named Yao Chengjie from Suzhou, who was in charge of the wine cellar. He wrote on the banners throughout the temple, then took the banners and dyed them black for mixed use. No one knew about it. After leaving his post and traveling only a few li, his son suddenly was possessed by the god on the boat. The god scolded him, saying: "I have blessed your market for a long time, the new and old banners are all mine, how dare you take them to make clothes for your son?" And pointed out which of his concubines ground the ink and which folded the banners, detailing the reasons. Everyone was shocked, Yao was afraid, and he hurriedly prayed to the god, promising to apologize, and his son then recovered.

Wu Zixu fled from Chu and served Wu. King Fuchai of Wu granted him the sword of Shu Luo and he committed suicide. His body was floated down the river, and thus he became a wave deity, known as Xutao, a name familiar to all. Today, there is a Xushan Township in Jiaxing, where the mountain is fifteen zhang high and two li in circumference. According to old records, Wu Zixu once campaigned in the area and conducted his operations there. The *Shui Jing* states that Wu Zixu died in Wu, and the people of Wu built a shrine by the river in his honor, naming it Xushan. The Wu Mountain in Hangzhou is also known as Xushan, and there is another Xushan in Wu County, Suzhou, indicating that the name is not unique. Nowadays, the Wu surname is very common in Xushan Township, and it is also said to be ancient. The *Jiahe Records* mentions that in the Gutinglin Temple, there is a shrine to the Righteous and Martial Public. Recently, the ground suddenly split open several feet, and there was a sound of wind and waves inside. When something was inserted, fire immediately ignited, and it remains so to this day. The Lusi Temple in Huating is also known as the Lusikong Temple. In the early years of the Yuanhe era, there were dozens of salt ships anchored in front of the temple. After a rain at night, there was a light like fire, spitting out or swallowing. The boatmen peered at it and saw an object several zhang long, as large as a house beam, with its mouth playing with a ball of fire. They pressed it with a bamboo pole, and it was startled and entered the grass. The light remained on the ground, and it was a pearl one cun in diameter. They wrapped it in clothing, and the light penetrated through. They then removed their undergarments to wrap it, and the light finally disappeared. Later, they went to Yangzhou to sell it and earned tens of thousands of strings of cash.

The *Yudi Zhi* records: During the Qin dynasty, Haiyan County was established, which Wang Mang later renamed Zhanwu County. The county subsequently became submerged in a lake, and on a small mountain within the lake grew a mulberry tree, hence the name. It is also said that during the Qin dynasty, a woman entered the lake and became a deity, which is why this temple was built. Wang Anshi's poem goes: "The name of the lake is inscribed in the mulberry forest, and the lotus leaves spread along the shore. What did the Qin woman do to become the lake's deity? Year after year, chickens and pigs are sacrificed, and the fishermen know the way. The mysterious and perilous nature of the lake, fortune and misfortune are easily deceptive." Wu Yuelong is the son of my friend Wu Zongli (Da Zhi). In the year Bimao, he and my other friends took the examination together. That year, they all passed the preliminary examination, with Wu Yuelong ranking as the second-place winner in the composition category, which was the eighth place in the overall results. On the night the results were announced, he dreamed of climbing a seven-story pagoda. Having reached the sixth level, with only one more level left, as he was about to ascend, he suddenly saw a person wearing a star-crowned cloud robe, resembling a celestial being, who shouted: "This is the Wild Goose Pagoda! Who are you to climb here?" The person chased him down, and he walked slowly to the outside of the pagoda, where he sat down and woke up in a start. When the final results were posted, he was listed outside the top seven. I personally witnessed this account. There was also Zhang Xiang, who also passed the preliminary examination in Bimao. Two days before the results were announced, he dreamed of someone holding a large insect for sale. Zhang Xiang struck it five times, and each strike produced a black mark, one of which spun and formed a character. Someone said: "Almost a complete success on the list." He was ranked first in the preliminary examination. Thus, it is said that success is predetermined and cannot be forced.

In the western wing of the Bai Gong Zhenwu Daoist Temple, there is a room housing a statue of the immortal Lu Dongbin, known as the Pure Yang True Person. The statue is extremely solemn and dignified, created by my great-uncle, Da Zhong Bao. The statue was originally placed in the Daoist hall, which was later abandoned. It was then moved and enshrined in this room, where it has become quite famous for its supernatural phenomena. Children sometimes come to worship and pray, asking for money, and occasionally find it on tables or where they sit, which is seen as evidence of the divine transformations of the immortal. In the village of Yi Feng, within the boundaries of Jiaxing County, there lived a Daoist named Wei Si Shi, who had a wife and children. In his middle age, he suddenly realized the emptiness of the world, abandoned his worldly life, and became a monk, practicing fasting and abstaining to an extreme degree. The villagers revered and respected him. In the winter of the third year of Chun You, he suddenly fell ill, assessing that his blood and qi were weak and he could not stand up. He called his companions to prepare a bath and change his clothes, saying, "The great limit has arrived; what do I have to cling to? Take care of yourselves." He then sat cross-legged and suddenly passed away. News of his passing spread far and wide, drawing crowds who looked at and admired him, sighing and envying his departure. For two days, the situation remained unresolved. In the spring of the year of Jia Shen of Chun You, I stayed at the home of the Shen family, where I temporarily resided. One night, I dreamed of a woman in red clothes arriving at the hall and carriage of my family. She had no maidservants and held a yellow silk skirt, directly entering the hall. In the morning, I told the students about the dream, but none of them understood its meaning. The next night, just as I was reading through books by the lamp, someone suddenly reported that there was a very urgent drum sound outside the street. Hurriedly, I sent someone to look, and it turned out that the market building had caught fire. The smoke and flames illuminated the sky. The collective effort put out the fire, just avoiding it from spreading to other places, only pulling down several small houses. Only then did I realize that the strange woman in the dream was a sign of the event.

To the west of Yongxing Bridge, the Lu family's residence has a large well, the depth of which is unknown and immeasurable, even during a severe drought, it does not dry up. Below it, one can row a boat, and sometimes, water plants and broken reed sails float up from it, though it is unknown where they come from. According to ancient legends, it is said that this well connects to the vast ocean. Could it be that the sea water flows underground through here? Now it has been acquired by the Fu family, and it is located right in the middle of the hall, covered with boards very carefully. This is to prevent falling in. During the peak period of my family, the eastern wing was used as a school, and the southwestern corner later became the residence of Wang, a resident. Wang saw that every night, a white-clad person appeared in his home, and he thought it was a ghostly presence. He guarded against it every night. One night, he chased it with a stick to the side of the stove, and it disappeared. When he dug, he found a bottle of silver. No one knew about it, and he used this to make a living, and eventually, he became moderately prosperous. Another person, Li, made a living by farming, initially very poor. One day, while swinging his hoe, he suddenly heard a sound from the manure. When he dug, he found a jar full of small gold plaques. Li obtained them and then traded them. Others discovered it and reported it to the authorities, and he was severely beaten. Half of the gold plaques were taken by others. Now, not a single one remains.

The imperial examination hall in Jiaxing was originally a state school, and there is still a Cangqing Bridge, with the old rules of the ritual water pool in place. Later, the school was moved to Fengchi Fang, and this site became the examination hall. At the end of each examination session, there are nearly two thousand candidates, along with those who carry their books and tools, and their families, totaling nearly ten thousand people. However, the third room in the western corridor, farthest to the north, is a place where candidates often die due to being possessed by demons. Sometimes it appears as a cat, and sometimes as a woman. Whenever there is a scream, the evil aura becomes even stronger. Therefore, those assigned to duties in this area often do not want to take them, and there have been several deaths over the years. Soldiers who stay in the corridors often see ghostly apparitions at night, and some even wake up from nightmares. To address this, an action was taken to help. In the year of Bingwu, the examiner had a dream in which someone identified himself as the Examination Hall General, saying that he died in this place and now serves as a spirit. He and his fellow spirits cause the deaths of candidates in the examination hall. He suggested building a temple in the northwest corner to honor him, and this would prevent such occurrences. The official informed the governor and a temple was built. As a result, the two examination sessions were relatively peaceful, and candidates preparing for the exams often brought money to pray for divine protection. Some say that this place was originally a judicial office, and during the chaos of the Xu Ming era, many people were wrongfully imprisoned and died here, which is why a three-sound shout is made.

In the southwest of Jiaxing County, sixty steps away, according to the *Geographical Records*, is the grave of Su Xiaoxiao, a renowned courtesan from the Jin Dynasty. Today, there is a small stone marker by the roadside, inscribed with the words: "Su Xiaoxiao's Tomb." In Xu Ning's *Cold Food Festival Poem*, he writes: "In the walls of Jiaxing, I meet the Cold Food Festival, and at sunset, every household returns to pay respects and sweep the graves. Only Su Xiaoxiao before the county gate, has no one to send her paper money and ashes." The Ziyin Temple is located west of Haiyan County and was originally known as Puming Temple. According to old records, General Dai Wei of the Jin Dynasty donated his residence to build the temple, and it was later renamed Guangxing Temple by the Duke of Sima, Wang Xun. In the second year of the Tianxi era (1018 AD), the temple was granted its current name. The temple houses a pagoda, which is extremely tall and majestic. Each level is illuminated by lamps, making it a beacon for sailors in the East China Sea. The pagoda serves as a landmark for them, and its grandeur is immense. There was a fisherman from the coast (whose name is not recorded) who went out to sea with his brothers to catch fresh fish. Suddenly, a violent storm arose, and their boat was destroyed. They all drowned in the sea. Their family wept day and night. One night, the wife dreamed that her husband returned and said: "Before I went out to sea, I had a dream where a deity warned me: 'There will be a storm tomorrow, do not go.' I did not believe it and so died here. When I first fell into the sea, I was carried away by the waves in the blink of an eye, hundreds of miles away. The deity tried to save me but could not reach me. Now I am in the Sea Tide Ghost Department, suffering greatly. Every day, the tides come in, and I am pushed and dragged along with others. All the Buddhist rituals and offerings are taken by other spirits, and I cannot benefit from them. Only the light from the lamps in the Ziyin Pagoda, which is a great source of merit and virtue, can help me." Her wife then sold her family's property and set up a lamp in the temple to pray for her husband. The next night, she dreamed that her husband came to thank her, saying: "Now I have been promoted to a higher level."

There are eighteen sea dykes in total. From the county to the sea, ninety-five li away, there is a town called Wanghai. After many years, the waves eroded the dykes, and all became the sea. During the Song Dynasty, the county magistrate Chen (name unknown) once built a Moon-Watching Pavilion five miles along the sea dyke. Nowadays, the base of the pavilion is in the water and can no longer be seen. Every year, the shoreline recedes by more than several feet, and not one of the eighteen sea dykes remains. The county seat is only about three hundred steps from the sea, and the Dushan area is annually inundated by saline tides, suitable for making salt, which causes suffering for farmers. The previous county magistrate (name unknown) personally supervised the laborers, moving the dyke several hundred steps inland and building another dyke called Gu Yue. Laborers found pottery jars and iron swords, thought they were strange, and smashed them all. During the reign of Emperor Qin Shi Huang, there was a fortune-teller who said that there is a celestial aura in the southeast. Therefore, he toured the eastern seas, and wherever he went, he buried gold and treasures to suppress the aura. Now, residents near the old county often dig up ancient artifacts. The area spans twelve li north to south and six to seven li east to west. To the southeast, it connects to the old county; to the southwest, it is close to the sea salt production area; to the east, it borders Guang戌; to the north, it borders Huating. Water transportation is convenient without interruption, and the land is remote with distant villages. According to the *Wu Di Zhi* (Records of Wu): Once, there was a golden ox that defecated gold, and the villager Gao Boting followed it. The ox entered a mountain cave, and people dug into the mountain to follow. The mountain collapsed, and both brothers died, and therefore, it was named the Golden Ox.

Mr. Zeng Zhi of San Mountain once stayed as a guest in the home of the Chen family. For seven years, there was no communication, and his scholarly pursuits had ceased, leaving him alone in one room. A Taoist came from Wu Mountain and said to him, "How long have you been homesick, and why not return?" Zeng replied, "The distance by water and land is three thousand miles; when will I arrive?" The Taoist cut paper into the shape of a horse, told him to close his eyes and get on the horse, sprinkled water on it, and it was as swift as the wind. The Taoist prayed, "You may return but do not stay long." In a moment, he arrived home, and the door and gate were as they were before. His wife ordered him to bathe and change into new clothes. Zeng said, "I will leave immediately." His wife said, "Just arrived and then left; why do not you think of your parents and family?" Zeng got on the horse and began to ride, but the horse's hoof broke, and he woke up, finding himself back in the study. All his clothes were newly made, and the Taoist was not seen, only leaving a medicine basket, inside which was a poem that said: "One ride like a dragon sends the guest back, with silver mane and green ears walking side by side. The fair lady does not allow a light parting; if not for the immortal, how could one know?" Jingde Zen Temple is five miles from the county, located outside the west gate of the city, near the cremation temple. In the past, there was a white hair several feet high, which the people considered a good omen, and they built a temple. There is a white dragon pond in front of the temple, because a white dragon dug a cave here, and many travelers were drowned. The residents built a pagoda and buried a golden sword to subdue it, and afterward, there was no more harm. Now people call it the Three Pagodas Bay Temple, dedicated to the Dragon King of Shunde. During the Chunxi era, there was a great drought, and County Magistrate Li Boshi reported the dragon-stirring incident to the governor. He tied a tiger bone to a long rope and lowered it into the dragon pond, and it rained. If it was taken too slowly, lightning surrounded the hall, and the situation became urgent, and someone was sent to retrieve it, and then it stopped.

My great-grandfather (Xun) was originally buried in Rishan, but later, through divination, a new site was chosen in Dingcun, and he was reburied there. A purple wisteria vine was tightly entwined around the coffin, and some people said that the presence of a purple wisteria vine at a burial site was a sign of good fortune. They cut the vine and moved the coffin, and from then on, the family gradually declined. In the northern gate of Jiaxing, there is a bridge called Child Bridge, with stone carvings of children at the four corners of the railings, hence its name. It is unknown when the bridge was built. After many years, it became a supernatural entity. Sometimes it would come out at night to knock on people's doors for food, or play in the market on moonlit nights. Many people saw it. One night, a brave person waited in secret until dark and saw several stone children slowly descending from the bridge. He shouted that there was a ghost and chased them with a knife, cutting off their heads. The supernatural occurrences then ceased. In the city of Xiuzhou, there is a tower called the Heavenly King Tower. During the reign of Emperor Gaozong (建炎), the Jurchen invaders (金虏) attacked, causing great turmoil in Suzhou and Xiuzhou, and they planned to massacre the people. A celestial king appeared on the city wall, as large as several houses, and the soldiers were terrified and retreated, sparing the entire region. After the chaos subsided, the tower was built in the northwest corner of the city, and it is still maintained today.

A monk named Wang Leiyin lived in a hermitage and was filial to the utmost. His mother fell gravely ill, and he prayed day and night to the deity he worshipped, the protector of the law. His sincerity moved the deity, who suddenly descended upon him, speaking in a foreign tongue: "I pity your filial piety and honesty, so I have saved your mother. I will teach you the medicine to cure her." Following the deity's instructions, his mother recovered. From then on, the deity often descended to him, frequently predicting the good and bad fortunes of people, drawing crowds from far and wide. One day, someone came to pray, and the monk was careless. The deity became angry and rebuked him, causing the monk to go mad and become uncontrollable. He demanded to be bathed, and his attendants, having no choice, prepared hot water and gave it to him. Even boiling water was not considered warm enough, and he threw himself into it, finding it pleasurable as he writhed around. The crowd knelt and begged for mercy, and the deity said, "I will lightly punish him." The monk was then spared. After the bath, his entire body was barely injured, and his illness had also healed. The deity no longer descended. The war and conflagration of the Song Dynasty left all places desolate. In Xuyi, a merchant stored a large jar of soy sauce and had already made a substantial profit. However, his greed grew, and he devised a plan to sell fake soy sauce, mixing it daily with salt water and fragments of broken tiles, coal, and other impurities. His profits multiplied tenfold. One night, during a storm, the roof beam of his house collapsed, and his wife and he were lying directly beneath it, both crushed to death. The jar of soy sauce was also broken. The neighboring houses, however, suffered no damage. Magistrate He wrote a poem: "How can a myriad of falsehoods compete with one truth? For a moment, they deceive those before them. The children are mostly mute, for retribution always has a cause. Can this not be believed?" ---

Lu Shiwu, a man from Huating, Jiaxing, lived in the Bamboo Forest Village. He made a living by catching turtles. Every day, he would catch turtles and return home, where he and his wife would live-boil the turtles before selling them. On the seventeenth day of the fourth month of the second year of Jia Tai (April 17, 1202, at 3-5 PM), a sudden gust of wind and heavy rain accompanied by lightning and thunder struck. Lu Shiwu and his wife and two daughters all died under the lightning axe, which symbolized divine punishment. Alas! Turtles and other aquatic creatures are divine beings in the water world. How can humans kill them? The retribution against Lu Shiwu is indeed terrifying. Recently, there have been people who enjoy eating turtles, inviting friends and family to gatherings to do so, calling these events "turtle-eating gatherings." They compete to see who can eat the most, showing a strong desire to kill living beings. How can they escape punishment? Hearing this, one should take heed! In Fengxin County, a villager tied a cow to a post, intending to kill it. The neighbor's son, who usually enjoyed feasting, became drunk and entered to watch. He sat down and pointed at the butcher, saying, "Quickly take the knife; I want raw liver and lungs. Don't delay, and don't give them to anyone else." He repeated this several times. Suddenly, the cow raised its head, glared with anger, and stared directly at the boy. With great effort, it broke the rope and rushed forward, piercing the boy's belly and carrying him away. The villagers, including the local gentry and their servants, numbering over a hundred, armed with spears and sticks and shouting, chased after the cow for forty miles until they finally obtained the boy's body.

Yan Tai, while traveling on the river, encountered a fishing boat and asked about it. The fishermen said there were fifty turtles. Tai used ten thousand coins to redeem and release them. After walking several dozen steps, the fishing boat capsized. That evening, fifty people came to Tai's door and told his parents: "Your son has deposited fifty thousand coins, which you can collect." The coins were all wet, and the parents found it strange. When Tai returned, he told about the strange incident of redeeming the turtles. Chen Hong Tai's family was wealthy. Someone borrowed one thousand coins from him, and Hong Tai demanded repayment very urgently. The borrower said: "Please don't worry, I will first raise more than ten thousand toads and sell them, which will be enough to repay you." Tai heard this and felt compassion, so he forgave the debt and gave him another ten thousand coins, instructing him to release all the toads into the river. After more than a month, Tai returned home at night, and his horse was startled and would not move forward. He looked ahead and saw a golden toad. The grandfather of the judge, Zeng Bozu (Xing Shu), was an orphan who grew up during the year of the rabbit. He took care of his mother, Xu, who was seeking medical treatment in Jiaxing, and stayed in a temple called Jingyan. When Xu Ming rebelled, causing chaos in the entire state, he only stopped at not killing monks. The mother and son knelt under the eaves of the temple, and many spears were thrust at them. Their lives were in danger, and they silently prayed to the Bodhisattva Ksitigarbha (the Earth Store Bodhisattva), asking for deliverance from the sword and spear, and for their descendants to never forget the incense offerings. They indeed escaped death and continue to worship at the ancestral grave of Lingyun. The judge's son, Zhu Bao (Guo), became a county magistrate, and Zhu Bao's son, Zhi Xian (Ji Ying), also passed the imperial examinations.

The Qiu family has long served the deity of Zou. During their prosperous times, the deity was extremely spiritual and mysterious. When people prayed to it, it could speak in human language, indicating one's fortune and misfortune, responding instantly. The family thus gradually prospered. Afterward, when the brothers divided and lived separately, the deity no longer spoke. To this day, the descendants of the Qiu family still practice shamanism without interruption. About three or four miles from East Lake is a village called Yangdun, where most of the inhabitants are named Yang. There was a man named Yang Siu who made a living by raising ducks. He would gather hundreds of ducks together, and when people sold ducks to him, he would collect them. Yang Siu benefited from the money, so he would always grab the ducks' necks and twist them on the ground, causing them to die immediately. He also learned a method to treat the ducks, pouring hot water over them while they were still alive, which caused them to lose their feathers. In his later years, he fell ill with a strange condition. He would often soak in a bathtub, and his wife and children would frequently add hot water, but he felt no heat. His skin became white and wrinkled. Later, someone stepped on his chest with their heel, and this mark still remains today. His family business became desolate. People believed that Yang Siu was suffering the punishment of boiling oil in hell.

The Wet Nurse of Fang the Magistrate and the Farmers of Niù In the village of Wèitáng in Xiù Prefecture, there lived a wet nurse named Zhōu, who was from Lín’ān. She was a straightforward and honest woman, confident and not afraid of being deceived. Whenever villagers asked for loans, she would lend them freely without regard for whether she knew them or not. There was a man named Cài who owed her the most money. Whenever she tried to collect it, he would always find excuses to avoid repayment. After several years, the wet nurse called him out and reproached him, but he would respond with false excuses, saying: "I indeed owe you money, and I have tried repeatedly to repay it, but it is always disrupted by official duties. Please give me more time. If you break the promise again this year, I will be reborn as an eight-udder female dog as repayment." Not long after, Cài died, and the Fang family got a dog with eight udders. The wet nurse often called out playfully, "Are you Cài?" The dog would wag its tail and come forward. From then on, whenever she called, the dog would come immediately. It lived for ten years before dying. In the first village of Déhuà Township in Jiāxīng Prefecture, there was a farmer named Niù Qī, who was engaged in farming. He often relied on his stubbornness to avoid responsibility and refused to pay the rent to his landlord. In the year Jiātài Xīnyǒu (1201), he planted eighty acres of early rice, all of which were harvested successfully. He stored the grain next to a stack of firewood, hidden without a trace. Despite this, he still filed a complaint with the authorities, claiming that his crops were damaged. In the middle of the night, the firewood and grain were burned to ashes. In the autumn of Rénxū (1202), his younger brother Niù Shí’èr also planted eighty acres of early rice, stored the grain at home, and complained about heaven and blamed the earth. Suddenly, at noon, the sky turned dark, and a strong wind swept the ground. His house caught fire, leaving nothing but ashes. Alas! Niù Qī and Niù Shí’èr cheated the officials and deceived people. The heavenly net is vast, though loose, it does not miss anything. This is also to be feared.

Gao, the Chief Clerk of Meishan, had a beloved son named Mei Lang, who was very intelligent. Unfortunately, he died young, and Gao deeply mourned him. One day, Gao suddenly died but revived, saying he had been taken to the underworld. At first, two officials came to summon him, leading him to a place that resembled a state capital or government office. Soon, he saw a person wearing a monk's robe and holding prayer beads come out. Gao looked closely and saw it was his father. His father rebuked him, saying, "You have acted unjustly in your duties. Did you know about this?" Gao asked, "What unjust actions have you taken?" His father replied, "In handling the case of murder at the relay station, you did not thoroughly investigate the matter and made the right wrong. Therefore, heaven took away your beloved son, Mei Lang, who is also here. You have good karma, and heaven has not yet taken your life. You are now returning to the world. You must be loyal to your ruler and obedient to your superiors, and you must not act for personal gain or make the right wrong. Abstain from killing, lust, anger, and wrath. If you follow my teachings, you will live out your natural lifespan. Otherwise, your lifespan and fortune will be reduced."

**Jiang Shiba, a lay Buddhist practitioner from Haiyan County, and Jiang Nian'er, a respected woman, daily recited Mahayana sutras and cut off their desires and passions. One day, they washed, brushed their teeth, changed their clothes, burned incense, and recited the Buddha's name, then wrote a verse and passed away.** **Jiang Shiba's verse:** This illusory body, Composed of the four elements. Today, it disperses, Each returns to its root. All illusions have vanished, Ashes fly and smoke disappears. Like the wind in the air, Like the moon in the blue sky. There is no obstruction, And it shines brightly. All is forever severed, There is no more to say. He also said: Walk the straight path, The heart does not deceive or twist. For forty years, I have freed myself from desires. I solely expound the Mahayana teachings, Read in the morning and evening. Today, waving goodbye and returning to the West, Naturally, the fruits are fully realized. **Jiang Nian'er's verse:** Having read the Lotus Sutra ten thousand four hundred times, Throughout life, there has been a karmic connection with incense and fire. The West is my path of return, Riding the boat of wisdom together with wind and moon.

The southern regions had been pacified, and in Jianzhou there was a general named Yu Hongjing. His wife, Zheng Shi, was of extraordinary beauty and was captured by rebel soldiers, who presented her to the subordinate general Wang Jianfeng. She was treated with impropriety, but Zheng Shi's resolve could not be swayed. Threatened with a sword, she did not yield. Wang Jianfeng then ordered to bring the other women he had captured and ordered Zheng Shi to kill and eat them. He said to her, "Are you afraid?" She replied, "Would I rather be sacrificed early to your kitchen? I swear that I will not be defiled by impropriety." In the end, he could not bring himself to kill her and presented her to the Grand General Zha Wenhui, intending to have sexual relations with her. Zheng Shi shouted, "The imperial army came to punish and restore order; righteous men and chaste women should be honored and rewarded. General Wang, the Minister of Works, came from the ranks of common soldiers, so it is not surprising that he behaves this way. But you, Sir, are well-read and a high-ranking general of the state. Why do you want to commit impropriety against a woman to satisfy your desires? I wish to be killed quickly." Zha Wenhui was greatly ashamed and sought out her husband and returned her to him. Zheng Shi's chaste and righteous conduct was awe-inspiring. The two generals, like tigers and wolves, ultimately dared not transgress. How can those women who are promiscuous and shameless look at this and not be ashamed?

Huang翁, a man from Huating, had been in the business of selling incense for generations. Later, he moved to the Willow Lane by East Lake, where his family continued to make a living selling incense. He would regularly travel to the banks of the Qiantang River in Lin'an to purchase fragrant woods, which he would process into incense and sell. The term "甜头" (tiantou) is a colloquial expression in the incense trade, referring to the fragrant woods imported from Hainan, such as sandalwood and agarwood. Huang翁 would cut these woods into thin slices, resembling coffin boards, and blend them with foreign incense. He would steam them thoroughly in a steamer, mix rice water with ink, and use a brush made from grass to evenly sprinkle the mixture onto the incense slices, creating a speckled pattern. After drying, he would sell the incense in the market. During the Chunxi era, one day Huang翁 set sail in a boat to return to Huating, anchoring his vessel at the mouth of East Lake for the night. The next morning, he planned to continue his journey. At the lake's entrance stood the Temple of the Great King of Mount Jinshān, which was revered by the local people. That night, at the third watch, a sudden figure grabbed Huang翁, beating him severely and accusing him: "What kind of evil deeds have you committed? You sold fake incense, so you must leave now." After several hours, he regained consciousness. The next day, he arrived home, fell ill for over a month, and eventually died. One night, his wife, Huang嫂, dreamt that she was in the underworld, where she saw two spirits washing a sinful man with boiling water from two buckets. One of the spirits then shouted at Huang嫂: "Old woman, this is your husband, Huang, who sold fake incense in life. Now he is suffering this punishment. When you return to the world, tell your sons to quickly abandon this trade." Upon waking, Huang嫂 wept bitterly and spoke to her sons. She immediately fed the monks and performed good deeds to try to save her husband, and the family subsequently changed their profession and engaged in a different means of livelihood.

In Haiyan County, there was a man named Ni who made incense from various types of wood. He would grind the wood into a fine powder, call it "print incense," and sell it. One night, he was burning mosquito-repellent incense when a small fire entered the basket of incense. The situation became urgent, so he used water to extinguish it. Nearby, there was also some sliced incense, which was also burned. The incense on the grinding stone caught fire again. Ni saw that the fire could not be stopped, and he immediately wanted to leave the house to save his life. But the entire room was filled with smoke, making it impossible to escape. Soon, the entire house was engulfed in flames and burned down.

Jiaxing Prefecture's Zhou Dalang, every time he sold incense, would bargain the price with others, and if someone doubted its quality, Zhou would say, "If this incense is not good, I swear that upon leaving, I will meet an evil spirit and be struck dead." He often used this oath as a word. During the Chunyou period, one day as he passed the bridge behind the prefecture, it was as if he encountered something that tripped him. People immediately supported him, but his breath had already ceased. Alas! The world burns incense, truly wishing to honor the sages and saints of the three realms and ten directions. Huang翁, Ni Sheng, and Zhou Dalang, by using discarded wood as if it were genuine and offending the deities with impurities, how could they not suffer punishment? There was someone who loved the Dao but did not know the way. He worshiped a withered tree morning and evening, always saying he was begging for immortality. Like this for twenty-eight years without tiring, one day the tree bore purple flowers, sweet dew like honey. Eating it, he immediately ascended to immortality. Huang Jue saw a Taoist at an inn drinking together. At the moment of raising the cup, the Taoist dipped his chopstick in wine and wrote the character "吕" on the table. Huang Jue realized it was Lü Dongbin and stood respectfully in awe. The Taoist then took out seven large coins and three small coins from his sleeve, saying, "The number cannot be increased." He also gave him a small amount of medicine. On New Year's Day, grind it in wine and take it; it can prevent illness for the whole year. As he said, he lived to over seventy, and the medicine also ran out. He wrote a poem saying, "The calendar on the bed shows no more days; counting the fingers, next year will be seventy-three." He passed away in that year.

Tchen Yuanzhi was known for accumulating unseen good deeds, and all birds were benefited by his kindness. Whenever he ate, hundreds of birds would fly and chirp to feed on the scraps. One night, he dreamed of a man in a red robe who said, "You have performed good deeds that benefit others, and your life was originally not to exceed forty years, but it has been extended to ninety-nine years, and you will die without illness." Emperor Shizong of the Later Zhou dynasty destroyed copper statues of Buddha, saying, "Buddhism teaches that giving one's head, eyes, marrow, and brain is beneficial to all beings, and one still has no regrets. Should we be attached to copper statues?" The Great Compassion copper statue in Zhenzhou was very powerful and responded to prayers. It was destroyed with axes and swords, splitting open its chest. Afterward, during his northern campaign, Emperor Shizong developed a boil on his chest, and it was said to be a form of retribution. Li, the local official, anchored his boat at night and washed his feet by the ship's side. Suddenly, something grabbed his foot in the water. The crowd tried to save him, but Li shouted, "The pain is unbearable, I can't bear it, I would rather die." He followed the creature into the water, and the next day, his body was not found. Jin Zhou Xing died and then came back to life, saying he had been summoned by the Heavenly Emperor. He ascended the throne and looked up, seeing purple clouds swirling above. The Heavenly Emperor's face was one foot square, and he asked those around him, "Is this Zhang Tian Di?" They replied, "The ancient Heavenly Emperor has long been revered, this is close to Emperor Ming of Cao Wei." Li Zhou's brother suffered from wind-related ailments, and someone suggested that snake wine could cure it. Li Zhou then sought a black snake, placed it alive in a jar, and fermented it with *qu* (a type of fermentation agent). For several days, the snake continued to make sounds. When the wine was ready, it had a strong and pungent aroma. Li Zhou drank it all down, and in a short while, it turned into water, leaving only the hair remaining.

In Moshan, there was a village boy herding cows. He washed his sweaty shirt and dried it on the grass. When the cow was grazing, it not only ate the grass but also the shirt. The boy's father suspected the neighbor's child of stealing the shirt and angrily said, "Having a son who steals, what use is he for?" He then threw the boy into the water. The neighbor's child cried out in innocence and called upon heaven. Just as the boy emerged from the water, his father threw him back in. Shortly after, a sudden thunderstorm struck and killed the cow. Miraculously, the shirt came out of the cow's mouth. Scholar Qian once met a person who told him, "Your punishment from heaven is imminent." He instructed the man to quickly apologize to the person involved. The man explained, "I have no other sins, only that I had been unhappy with my food and discarded it in the ditch." Qian advised him to quickly retrieve and eat the discarded food. The man washed away the dirt from the food. A loud thunderstorm occurred. Qian told him to quickly eat the food along with the dirt. The thunderstorm stopped. In Huizhou, a prostitute was struck dead by lightning in the marketplace. There was a red inscription on her side: "Li Linfu, through his cruel manipulation of power, was punished by the emperor, who ordered this woman to be struck by lightning. She is said to be the reincarnation of a previous life." This incident occurred on a certain day in the sixth month of the sixth year of the Yuanhe era. The golden silkworm is a mythical creature, golden in color, that eats Shu brocade. Its droppings are toxic and can kill if placed in food or drink. The silkworm can bring wealth and make people rich quickly, but it is very difficult to get rid of, even with water, fire, or weapons. People often hide the silkworm in boxes filled with gold and silver and throw them into corners of the road. Someone might pick up the box and take it away, calling it "marrying a gold silkworm."

In the Huili Temple of Linjiang Army, a revolving sutra repository was initially constructed, and monks were required to contribute 1,000 coins to turn the wheel once. A courtesan, who had lost her husband and was extremely poor, wished to turn the wheel to gain merit for the afterlife. After months of hard work, she collected donations but never reached the required amount. Faced with poverty and the inability to sustain herself, and with her impending marriage, she could not bear to give up. She carried the money she had collected, wept in front of the sutra repository, threw the coins onto the ground, and bowed in prayer. Miraculously, the wheel turned on its own, shocking the entire temple. From then on, the restriction on the number of turns was no longer enforced. There was a young man named Zhao who was cooling off by the water and saw a traveling merchant scooping water to rinse his mouth. The merchant bent over the pond, and a ghost emerged from the water, reaching up to his neck, three times advancing and three times stopping. Zhao cried out, and the ghost disappeared. The merchant said, "There is a small amount of realgar in my hair, which has the effect of warding off evil spirits." In Nanyang, there was a man named Hou Qing who owned a copper statue and wanted to sell his cow to gild it. However, he had already used the money from the cow for an urgent matter. One night, his wife dreamed that the statue said, "You and your wife owe me gold, and have not paid for a long time. Now I will take your son to settle the debt." The next morning, the son fell ill, and the statue suddenly turned golden, its light illuminating the surrounding area, drawing the attention of the neighbors. In Lingling, the governor's daughter was attracted to her father's secretary but had no way to be with him. She had her maid drink the leftover water that the secretary had consumed, and as a result, she became pregnant and gave birth to a son. The governor did not know the child's origin. One day, he sent the boy to find his father. The boy entered the secretary's tent and transformed into water. The secretary was greatly shocked and questioned his daughter, who then revealed the truth. The governor eventually married his daughter to the secretary.

A person who enjoyed eating sheep heads once went out in the morning and saw a being with a sheep's head and a human body, dressed in splendid attire. The being said, "I am a god without a fixed position, and my domain is among sheep. You have eaten many sheep heads, so I have come to take you. If you stop eating them, I will spare you. Otherwise, I will kill you." The person was terrified and stopped eating sheep heads thereafter. In the west of Leizhou, there is a temple dedicated to the Thunder God. The people annually offer a thunder drum cart. Someone who ate yellow fish with pork was immediately struck by a thunderbolt. Whenever there is a big thunderstorm, people often dig up a stone called {石}, which is known as Thunder God ink and shines like lacquer. In the two caves of Gongshan in Wuyuan, there is a cave. At the end of the Xiantong era, a Daoist named Zheng descended into the cave with a rope. There was light nearby, and he ventured further. The path ended, and water blocked the way. On the other side of the river, there were flowers and trees, and two Daoists were playing chess. They sent a boy to row a boat to Zheng and asked if he wanted to cross. Zheng replied, "I will return." The boy rowed back, and Zheng climbed back up the rope. The next day, a stone shoot blocked the entrance of the cave, and since then, no one has entered again. In Zhongnan Mountain, there was a person who had no clothes and was covered in black hair. He could fly and was unreachable. He was captured by a hunter, who said he was a person from the Qin palace who had fled to the mountains during a time of chaos. An old man taught him to eat pine nuts. At first, they were very bitter and sour, but later, he got used to them and never felt hungry. The hunter fed him with grains, which at first, he found very smelly. He vomited for several days until he felt better. His body hair fell out, and he gradually aged and died.

Zhu Shigu, a native of Meizhou, fell ill at the age of thirty and could not eat. Upon hearing or smelling meat or fish, he would vomit. He used a fire pan to cook thin soup and pour it over plain rice, eating it frequently, but doctors could not cure him. Historical records note: "Those who do not read medical texts but attempt to treat others are truly pitiable." His illness is described in the *Suwen* (a part of the *Huangdi Neijing*), known as "food hang." In humans, the lungs have six lobes, which expand like an umbrella, covering the spleen. When the mother (lungs) and child (spleen) energies are harmonious, one can eat. If there is any discord, the lungs cannot expand, and the spleen is blocked, thus one does not enjoy eating. The *Suwen* states: "The lungs' lobes are affected by heat and hang." A remedy was given, and after buying and taking the medicine, after three days he could smell the fragrance of cooked meat, took and ate it, and recovered. During the end of the Qingli era, Ouyang Xiu, a renowned scholar and statesman, stayed overnight by the water at the Cai Shi ferry. The boatman was sound asleep, and it became dark. Ouyang extinguished the candle and was about to sleep when he faintly heard a voice calling, "Go away." The stern of the boat responded: "There is a ship of a high-ranking official docked here; we cannot leave without bringing food supplies." Privately, Ouyang thought: "The stern of the boat is close to the shore, and there are no people around; it must be a ghost." He stayed awake all night. At dawn, he heard the sound of galloping horses on the shore. The stern of the boat said: "Please bring back the food supplies and let us go." The response was: "The place is not clean, and we cannot return." Ouyang found this strange. On a later day, he visited Jinshan Mountain and spoke with Elder Ruixin, who said: "On that night, a donor set up a water and land offering and brought his family. While performing the ritual, he suddenly felt sleepy. Soon after, he gave birth to a child. Soon after, a stench wind extinguished the candles, and everyone was terrified." It was the same night Ouyang stayed at the Cai Shi ferry. Ouyang eventually participated in major state affairs.

Pian Yuandu traveled to Yuhang, mooring his boat at Si Zhou. A monk or deity emitted light that illuminated his boat, and ten thousand people looked up in admiration. Officials were informed that Pian Yuandu would not rise again, and he passed away in Gaoyou. People say that Pian Yuandu was the reincarnation of Mu Yi. A person found a blue stone, as large as a brick, with a nose-like feature, an iron chain passing through it, and a length of several zhang, with no breaks in the chain. A sea merchant saw it and exchanged it for a large sum of money, claiming that it harmonizes with metal and stone. The stone was hung in the sea, pulled out through the night, and had gold on top. A foreign monk from the Western Regions could control life and death. Emperor Taizong ordered brave and strong people to test this ability. The tested individuals died and revived as predicted. Fuyi said, "This is an evil method. Evil does not harm the righteous. If he curses me, it will not work." He summoned the monk to curse him, but initially felt nothing. The monk fell down and did not revive again.

During the Tianfu period, there was a severe famine in the region of Longyou. That year, the autumn harvest was very abundant, but just before the harvest, most of the crops had no ears of grain. People began searching in the fields near mouse holes, and many found a significant amount of grain. Some families dug through every hole, and some even found five or seven hu of grain. It was said that this hidden grain was called a "mouse granary," and the starving people went out to find food, saving many lives. In Yiling, there were yin and yang stones. The yin stone was always moist, while the yang stone was always dry. During a drought, people would beat the yin stone, and during rainy weather, they would beat the yang stone, and both would respond. Wei Sixuan sought the alchemy of gold. One day, a recluse named Xin Rui came to visit him. Xin Rui had a severe abscess that was bleeding profusely. Wei was hosting guests when Xin Rui suddenly urinated on the banquet table. The guests were angry and left, and Xin Rui also took his leave. Suddenly, he disappeared. When they looked at his urine, it was a purple liquid, shining brightly. One of the guests said, "Xin belongs to the metal element, and the West belongs to the metal element. Could Xin be the essence of gold?" In the small Yu Mountain of the South Sea, there was a ghost mother who gave birth to a thousand ghosts in the morning and ate them in the evening. Today, the ghost mother deity in Cangwu has a tiger's head and a dragon's feet, with a python's eyes and mosquito-like eyebrows. Li Yu, the military officer in Jingnan, was gravely ill and was taken to the underworld. There, a person who had already died asked, "How could the imperial attendant come here?" Someone in the village claimed to have seen a person enter Li Yu's home, where his wife and children were weeping around him. He lay there for a day, and people said he had been substituted. Wang Zhu went to a temple to escape the heat and saw an old man with a hunchback and protruding ribs. He said, "This is the place." The next day, when Wang Zhu looked again, it was a camel. Could what he saw the previous night have been the spirit of the camel?

闲窗括异志 宋 鲁应龙 海盐县本武原县，在秦属会稽，汉因之。《地理志》云：故武原有塘官。《寰宇记》云：广斥盐田相望。《汉地理》：县有盐官。东出五里有武原，王莽改为展武县。后陷为湖（拓湖是也），移于武原乡，改武原县，后改海盐县。汉安帝二年，又陷（当湖是也），移故邑城为故邑县。又移于海塘，为海塘县。唐景云二年，属苏州。先天二年废。开元五年复置。晋天福四年置秀州，遂属焉。县有向武原十八都十九都。然十八都分上下村。一在十八里堰下，与齐景交界；一在当湖，自湖首至界泾桥，与嘉兴交界。以德藏寺基及道院底籍都。十七都自有大易，十七都不应有二。况地理相接不同，不知何名。今止以上下十八都为定云耳。
当湖在今县北五十里，南北十二里，东西六里。古老相传：地初陷时，有妇人产一物，若蛟蜃状。濯于水，遂陷一方，迤<辶里>从东北去。今有泖港，直通太湖。昨得石刻，乃唐吴郡陆府君墓铭，葬于苏州海盐县齐景乡当湖。则当湖之名旧矣。或云：鹦鹉洲图经不载，岂县未陷曾有此湖耶？曩岁渔者于湖中获一铁链，长不计极，舟满几覆，惧而弃之。或云系蜃于此。自汉迄今，上下千馀年，湖日浅，土日增，闻有于其中仿佛见其馀趾。

金山忠烈王汉博陆侯姓霍氏，吴孙权时一日致疾，黄门小竖附语曰：国主封界华亭谷，极西南有金山咸塘，湖为民害，民将鱼鳖食之，非人力能防。金山故海盐县。一旦陷没为湖，无大神护也。臣汉之功臣霍（某）也，部党有力，能镇之。可立庙于山。吴王乃立庙，建炎间建行宫于当湖，赐名。显应尤著，乡民祈祷辄应，部下钱侯尤为灵著。王以四月十八诞辰，浙之东西，商贾舟楫，朝献踵至。自入四月至中旬末，一市为之鼎沸。闻有设祭于松柏间，祀其先亡，恸哭而反。谓之小岳庙，庙中铁铸四圣，由海而来，至今见存。

绍兴间方腊叛，浙右惊恐。时鲁氏居东武，家尚单微。溃卒之留于苏秀，奔走四出，村落居民大不安。鲁氏家事真武，极灵。因祷，谋远迁以避难，神不许。未几，溃卒将及境，忽空中有黑旗现，瞑雾四合。卒大恐，引去，阉境获免。遂于市西建道院事之，至今有祷辄应。 五显灵官大帝，佛书所谓华光如来，显迹婺源久矣，岁岁朝献不绝。淳中乡人病于涉远，乃塑其像，迎奉于德藏寺之东庑，建楼阁居之。祠之前素有井，人无汲者，自立祠后，人有汲其水饮之者，病辄愈。由是汲者，祷者日无虚焉。寺僧利其资，每汲一水则必令请者祷于神，得茭杯，吉，然后汲水，并以小黄旗加之上。自是请者日少，今亦不复验矣。 古老相传：湖初陷，白沃史君跃马疾走不及，遂驻马以鞭指，得湖东南一角，水至不没，因立庙。迨今此地独高。又云兄弟三人，一在沙腰，一在乍浦，皆称白沃庙。

德藏寺本宝兴寺，唐会昌元年置，光启初废。后唐清泰中，乡民丘邰即故基新之。大中祥符改今名，时改寺为宫，宫建炎复为寺，寺有两华亭，寺有水陆斋坛，往岁富民沈忠建。每设斋，施主虔诚则茶现瑞花。故花俨然可睹，亦一异也。 东林施水院本定庵居士白莲道场，寺有藏，岁久弊甚。住持僧智祥力鸠众缘为之，仅成规模，其中实无所有。始寺有转藏，不问多寡，僧以一饼啖之，由是至者甚众。人有病祟，必以东林藏转之即愈。盖寺有神姓施，封护国公，为之打供，僧徒得以济。 齐景乡县北四十里有庙在焉，图宅号齐景公庙，一云未明大王。古老相传：齐景公遵海而南，观于转附朝亻舞，曾游于此，立庙于斯。旧有碑，今磨灭不存矣。唐贞元十四年，太子左赞善大夫吴郡陆使君夫人汝南县君周氏墓志云：于嘉兴县，东界海盐县齐景乡青墩原西北茔。则齐景乡青墩之名旧矣。

元丰末，秀州人家屋瓦霜后冰自成花，每瓦一枝，正如画家所为。折枝有大花如牡丹花叶者，细花如萱草海棠者，皆有枝叶，无毫发不具，虽巧笔不能为之。以纸摹之，无异石刻。 宝圣石佛院在嘉兴县东南，唐至德二年于寺基掘得石佛四躯，至今见存。天圣中赐名宝圣，人但呼石佛寺（宝一作保） 。 庚子岁夏旱，湖间可以通轨。有渔舟夜舣水浒，遥见有光烛。人意谓必窖藏，遂于中夜掘之，得砖一井，片长六七寸，两首各有方窍相入，两面皆有手掌纹，极细，宛然可见。不知此砖始于何时。窃意当时陶人手法为之耳。儿童争鬻于市，或取以为砚，清润细腻可爱。余尝得片砖，为好事者取去。

南林祖茔，高祖宣羲之墓。尝闻诸伯叔祖言：初营地时，高祖颇明地理，将凿池引水至墓之西南，夜梦一妇人请曰：妾有墓在正南所开池处，君戒役夫勿伤吾墓，当有厚报。次日果于其地得金数块，遂用以营寺。至今其墓尚存，自建创以来将逾百年，林木坞竹视他处为盛。丙午夏忽生双笋数株，莫不嗟异，各有赋咏。然竟不成竿，亦无他应。岂物反常为妖，卒以自毙云。 光严庵正议之茔濒湖占胜，为一方冠，东南皆□湖，远峰列如笔架，一塔屹于波心。文锋挺立，登名仕版者世有其人，视他族为最盛。淳间，忽树间出烟一道，远近莫不惊异。有细视之者，见其间有蠓蚋不可计，从树中出，终日不绝。盖此烟即此所成，不知何异。

湖心有地一方，立塔以按风水，人呼之曰按山。湖水弥滂，时盗多窜伏于此。由是守庵者不敢居，遂成荒芜。其中有大穴如瓮，下极空洞，巨蟒潜伏于内。时有人见之，或偃卧湖沙之侧。近年有数道者居之，佛殿廊庑稍稍成绪，蛇亦不复见矣。余家旧有蛇穴于壁间，每春月，常有小蛇出没，近岁稍少。又有一族人课仆锄草，忽闻地中有声，入土尺许，有石板盖覆甚固，启之得缸，可贮数石米。其中皆巨蛇八九，奔走四出，急击之，或死或窜去，竟不知从何而入也。意者必有异物，盖不遇而变化云。 陈山在县东北四十里，高八十一丈，周围一十五里。有白龙湫显济，敷泽龙王庙。山顶有龙穴，深不数尺，春夏不涸，百姓遇旱则祷于穴，必有异物见。因取其水祀之，雨即滂沛。又有龙母冢在焉，每岁常在七月多风雨，人谓龙洗墓云。

陈山龙王庙后有观音殿，曩年忽有两石从半山斗坠而下，一从殿后壁滚入观音座下，一坠殿之西。屋瓦无所损，不知从何而入殿中也。今二石尚存，亦可异。留题者甚多。余乙卯岁到祠下，尝赋诗于壁以纪其事。 华亭县北七十里有氵殿湖，山上有三姑庙。每岁湖中群蛟竞斗，水为沸腾，独不入庙中。神极灵异，寺僧藉其力以给斋粥，水陆尤感应。向年有渔舟舣湖口，忽见一妇人附舟，云欲到氵殿山寺，及抵岸，妇人直入寺去，舟中止遗一履。渔人执此履以往索渡钱，寺僧甚讶之，曰：此必三姑显灵。因相随至殿中，果见左足无履，坐傍百钱在焉。遂授渔人而去。嘉禾百咏云：神居阴阳护，寻□捍洪波。莫虑蛟龙怒，年来畏叱呵。

德藏寺前钟乃铜所铸，音极洪响。尝见古老云：初铸钟时，有匠者云此钟未可便扣，俟吾至六十里乃击之。及既去，方至新坊十八里寺僧遽扣之，匠人闻其声叹曰：声止于此。今寺中钟自新坊十八里外不复闻矣。怪哉！ 当湖酒库有四圣庙在炊淘之后，立祠以来阅岁滋久。前后交承祀之，奉之甚谨。每一任初到，则上两幡；既解印，则复两幡。酬神之庇，以为定例。丙辰丁巳之间，有姑苏姚承节（应瑞）者，董糟丘，将幡书遍于神祠中，然后取幡染为黑色杂用。人无知者。及去任未数里，忽其子舟中为神所凭，责之曰：我立祠福汝坊场久矣，新旧之幡皆我之物，安得擅取以为儿服耶？及指其妾何人磨墨，何人折幡，历道其所以。众皆惊愕，姚惧，亟祷于神，许以谢过，其子遂苏。

伍子胥逃楚仕吴，吴王赐以属镂之剑自杀，浮其尸于江，遂为涛神，谓之胥涛，人皆知之。今嘉兴有胥山乡，山高一十五丈，周围二里，旧经云：伍子胥伐邀，经营于此。《水经》云：子胥死于吴，吴人立祠江上，名胥山。杭州吴山亦名胥山，苏州吴县亦有胥山，则其名非一。今胥山乡伍姓甚繁，亦谓之云古。 《嘉禾志》：顾亭林庵中有忠烈公祠，近岁忽地裂数尺，中有风涛声，以物探之，应手火起，至今尚然。 华亭陆四官庙一名陆司空。元和初，有盐船数十艘于庙前泊，夜中雨过，有光如火，或吐或吞。船人窥之，见一物长数丈，大如屋梁，口弄一团火，以竹篙抑之，惊入草际，光遗在地，乃一珠径寸。以衣裹之，光透出。乃脱亵服裹之，光始不见。后至扬州卖之，获数万缗。

《舆地志》：秦置海盐县，王莽改为展武县，县陷为湖，湖中小山生柘树，因以为名。又云秦时有女入湖为神，即此祠也。荆公诗云：柘林著湖名，菱叶蔓湖滨。秦女亦何事，能为此湖神。年年赛鸡豚，渔子自知津。幽妖屈险阻，祸福易欺人。 吴跃龙，余友吴宗礼（达之）之子也，乙卯与余友乡举同廊就试。是岁俱发小荐，而跃龙实为亚榜赋魁，实通榜词赋之第八也。揭晓之夕，梦登七层宝塔。已及六层，止馀一层，欲上之间，忽见一人星冠云帔，若天尊像，叱曰：此雁塔也，汝何人辄登此！连步逐下，迤逦至塔外，遂坐其傍，惊而寤。及榜至，乃在七名之外。余亲见其说。又有张湘亦以乙卯魁亚荐，揭晓两夕前，梦人持巨{般虫}扑卖。湘一扑五钱皆黑，一钱旋转不己，竟作字。一人曰：几乎浑纯及榜。乃为小荐第一。功名前定不可强求也如此。

百宫真武道院西庑一室有纯阳真人吕翁像，极端严，乃曾叔祖大中所创。道堂中塑像，道堂废。遂移奉于此，颇著灵异。小儿有拜祷乞钱者，或于几上及坐处得之，亦见其仙道变化之验云。 嘉兴县界移风乡有魏四十道者，有妻有子，中年忽悟真空，舍俗出家，修行斋戒甚至。乡民敬之重之。淳丙辰冬，忽感疾，自度气血衰，不能起，呼同侣具汤沐更衣曰：大限到来，吾复何恋，各自珍重。遂跏趺奄然而逝。远近闻者肩踵相摩，观瞻叹羡，凡两日未定。 淳甲申春，余馆于沈氏书垫，因寓宿焉。一夕梦妇人着红衣至其家厅庑下轿，无侍女，手执黄罗裙，直入其堂。旦与诸生言之，皆莫晓所谓。次夕方{堇}灯披阅卷帙，忽有人报街外鼓声甚急，仓皇使人视之，乃市楼失火。烟焰烛天，众力扑救，仅免延燎，止拽倒小屋数间。方知妇人之怪也。

永兴桥之西陆氏宅有大井，不知何年所凿，面阔数尺，其深不可测，虽大旱不涸。其下可以转篙，时时于其中有浮萍及破碎蒲帆浮起，不知何来。古老相传云：此下通大海。岂海水伏流地中从此过耶？今为富氏得之，正居堂之中，以板覆盖甚谨。盖防颠溺也。 余家全盛时，以东庑为书塾，其西南隅后为居民王氏宅。王见其家每夜常有白衣人出现，意其为祟，每夜防之。一夕持杖逐至灶侧而没，掘之得银一瓶。人无知者，遂以此经营，他之，遂小康焉。又李园者以种圃为业，初甚贫。一日挥锄，忽粪土中有声，掘得一瓮，皆小金牌满其中。李得之，遂转而货易，为他人所发，闻于官，备极笞楚，半为他人所得。今无复一存矣。

嘉兴贡院元是州学，今有采芹桥，泮水之旧规在焉。后迁学于凤池坊，此地遂为贡院。每举终场几二千人，荷笈而进者，随子弟而入者，几及万馀人。然西廊之第三间极北，举子常有为魅所凭而至死者。或如猫而过，或如妇人，每一发喊则妖气愈盛。是以分案于其间者，多不欲就。前后所死非一。兵卒之宿于廊庑，往往夜见鬼物，甚至惊魇不醒。遂□□救。丙午岁将赴举，监试官忽梦有人自称贡院将军，云我死于此地，今得为神。每举子死于场屋者，皆我辈为之。可立庙于西北隅事我，则免于是。明言于府以立祠焉。由是两举稍安，士人之就试者，莫不先期备金钱，祷以求阴庇。或云此地元为勘院，徐明之乱，多鞠死于此，故遇呼喊三声则出矣。

嘉兴县西南六十步，《地志》云晋歌妓苏小小墓。今有片石在道畔厅，曰：苏小小墓。徐凝《寒食诗》云：嘉兴郭里逢寒食，落日家家拜扫归。只有县前苏小小，无人送与纸钱灰。 资圣寺在海盐县西，本普明院。旧记晋将军戴威舍宅为寺，司徒王询建为光兴寺。天禧二年赐今名。寺有宝塔，极高峻，层层用四方灯点照。东海行舟者皆望此为标的焉，功为甚宏。有海滨业户（某）与兄弟泛舟入洋口接鲜，风涛骤恶，舟楫悉坏，俱溺于海而死。其家日夕号泣，一夕梦其夫归曰：我未出海时，先梦神告曰：来日有风波之厄，不可往。吾不信，遂死于此。初坠海时，弹指随波已去数百里，神欲救我不可及。今在海潮鬼部中，极苦，每日潮上，皆我辈推拥而来。他佛事祭享，皆为诸鬼夺去，我不可得，独有资圣塔灯光明，功德浩大耳。其妻因鬻家赀，入寺设灯愿，次夕又梦夫来谢云：今得升一等矣。

捍海塘凡十八条，自县去海九十五里有望海镇，岁久波涛冲啮，尽为洋海。绍兴中，知县陈（某）尝于海塘五里建望月亭，殆今则亭基在水中，不可复见。每岁沙岸何止数尺，十八条捍海岗岸无一存者。县治去海无三百步，而独山一带岁岁咸潮透入，可以晒卤，耕种者苦之。前政史宰（卿）亲督畚钟，移入数百步，别筑一塘谓古岳。瓦盎及铁剑役夫得之，以为奇，举手皆粉碎。秦始皇时有望气者言，东南有天于气。故东游海上，所至埋金宝以厌胜之。今故邑近海居民多掘得古物。 当湖南北十二里，东西六七里，东南则通故邑，西南则近海盐，其东则广戌，其北则华亭接境，舟楫便利无间断，地迥村远。 《吴地志》：昔有金牛粪金，村民皋伯通随之，牛入山穴，凿山而入。山颓，兄弟皆死，因名金牛。

三山曾先生（陟）尝寓馆于陈氏，七载音信不通，夏尸青衿俱歇，独处一室。有道人自吴山来，谓之曰：于思乡之切，何不少称归？陟曰：水陆三千里，几时得到？道人剪纸为马，令合眼上马，以水巽之，其疾如风。祝曰：汝归不可久留。须臾到家，门户如旧。妻令入浴易新衣，陟曰：我便去。妻曰：才归便去，何不念父母妻子乎？陟便上马而行，所骑马足折，惊寤，乃身在书馆中。随身衣服皆新制者，道人亦不见，惟留一药篮，中有一诗云：一骑如龙送客归，银鬃绿耳步相随。佳人未许轻分别，不是仙翁岂得知。 景德禅院去县五里，在城西门外之焚化院。昔有白毫高数尺，民以为祥，乃作寺。有白龙潭在寺前，以白龙穴于此，行人多漂溺，居人作塔埋金剑镇之，后遂无害。今人谓之三塔湾寺，三伽蓝顺德龙王也。淳熙大旱，知县李伯时以搅龙事告太守，以长绳系虎骨，缒于龙潭中，遂得雨。取之稍迟，雷电绕厅，事亟，令人取之，乃止。

上舍伯祖（巽）旧葬惹山，后忽卜兆于丁村，遂迁葬焉。其中紫藤蟠固棺上，或云：穴有紫藤，此吉征也。遂斫藤迁之，自后其家浸衰。 嘉禾北门有孩儿桥，桥栏四角皆石刻孩儿，因名之，不知何时所建。岁时既久，遂出为怪，或夜出叩人门户求食，或于月夜游戏于市。人多见之。一夕有胆勇者至夜密伺，果见其三二石孩儿徐徐自桥而下，遂大呼有鬼，以刀逐至其处，斫去其头，怪遂绝。 秀州子城有天王楼，建炎间金虏犯顺，苏秀大扰，将屠之。有天王现于城上，若数间屋大，兵卒望之怖惧，遂引去，一州之境获免。及乱平，建楼西北隅，见今事之。

有住庵僧王了因，事母至孝，母病危笃，日夜祷于所事韦天护法神。诚意感格，忽神降其身，作蛮语云：悯汝孝诚，故救汝母。教以药饵，遂愈。自是，神常降之，言人休咎多验，远近趋之。一日有人请祷，僧不谨，神怒责，遂发狂不可止。索浴左右，不得已具汤，与之汤。百沸犹以为冷，投于中，宛转为快。众拜祈哀，神曰：姑薄惩之尔。遂免。及出浴，举体略无少损，病亦愈。神不复降矣。 绍兴兵火之变，所在荒凉。盱眙有市人储酱一瓮，获利已多。然贪心愈生，设计售伪，日以咸水及碎瓦屑炭煤之属和之，所得十倍。一夕风雨，屋栋桁折，而夫妇正卧其下，皆压死，瓮亦破焉。而旁舍略无损动。何提刑诗云：万伪何缘斗一真，时间谩得面前人。生男种女多喑哑，果报元来必有因。可不信哉！

卢十五，嘉兴华亭人，所居修竹乡。卢十五以捉鳖为业，每捉鳖归舍，与妻其活煮其鳖，然后出卖，每日如是。嘉泰二年壬戌四月十七申时，忽大风骤雨，雷电闪光，霹雳大震，卢十五并妻女三人皆死雷斧之下。呜呼！鼋鼍龟鳖水族中之灵物也，人岂可杀乎？卢十五之报亦可畏也。近有食鳖之人，心既好食，又招宾友聚会而食，号团鱼会，彼此以所食多寡为胜负，杀生之念滋甚，罪报何逃，闻此可不戒哉！ 奉新县村民絷牛于柱，将杀之。其邻家子平时饕食，乘醉入观，踞坐指屠者曰：速操刀，我欲肝肺生食，不宜缓，仍不可与他人也。语至再三，牛忽骧首怒目，直视此子，奋力掣索断，直前径触之，穿其腹，戴之以走，过四十里不脱。乡民及豪家子弟、僮奴极百馀人，枪杖叫噪，共往追逐，乃得其尸。

严泰江行逢渔舟，问之，云：有龟五十头。泰用钱十千赎放之，行数十步，渔舟乃覆。其夕乃有五十人诣泰门，告其父母曰：贤郎附钱五十千，可领之。缗皆沾湿，父母怪之。及泰归，乃说赎龟之异。 陈宏泰家富于财，有人假贷钱一万，宏泰征之甚急。其人曰：请无虑，吾先养蛤蟆万馀头鬻之，足以奉偿。泰闻之恻然，已其偿，仍别与钱十千，令悉放之江中。经月馀，泰因夜归，马惊不进，前视之，乃一金蛤蟆也。 司法曾伯祖（行恕）卯角而孤，侍母徐氏就医嘉兴，留精严僧舍。值徐明反，扰乱一州，止不屠僧。母子俯伏于寺之夹衡下，众枪攒刺，命在须臾，默祷伽蓝神资善兴福明王，愿脱刀兵之难，世世子孙不忘香火，果得免死，至今奉事于灵云祖茔。司法生主簿（果），主簿生知县（季颖），相继登科。

巫家丘氏世事邹法主，其家盛时，神极灵异。人有祷之者，能作人语，指其祸福，感应如响。家遂稍康。自后兄弟析居，神亦不复语，今其子孙尚以巫祝相传不绝。 去东湖三四里有村曰杨墩，左右皆杨其姓者。有杨四九者以养鸭为生，数百为群，人有鬻之者，就令其打并。杨利于得钱，则每鸭必执其颈，碇以宛转于地，立死，前后不知其几矣。又得寻治之法，沃之以热汤而气未绝，随寻而身毛脱落。晚年得一疾，甚怪。每常浸浴缸中，妻孥频频添汤，极热而不觉，皮肤皆浸成白折。又令人以足跟踏心，至今尚存，而家事索然矣。人以为杨生活受镬汤地狱报云。

秀州魏塘村方通判乳媪周氏，临安人，为人朴直，自信不虑人欺。村民或从假贷，不问识不识，随意与之。有蔡公者负最多，每督取，率托以他故。经数年，媪呼而责之，每以妄言答云：实负婆钱，累欲偿，辄为官事所荡，愿更宽，今岁如再背约，当为八乳牝狗以报。未几蔡死，而方家得一犬八乳。周媪常戏呼曰：汝是蔡公耶？即掉尾而前，自是闻呼即至，十年乃死。 嘉兴府德化乡第一都钮七者，农田为业，常恃顽抵赖主家租米。嘉泰辛酉岁，种早禾八十亩，悉以成就收割，囤谷于柴齐之侧，遮隐无踪，依然入官诉伤，而柴与谷半夜一火焚尽。壬戌岁秋，其弟钮十二亦种早稻八十亩，藏谷于家，又且怨天尤地。忽日午间天宇昏暗，大风卷地，其家一火，灰烬无馀。呜呼！钮七、钮十二欺官瞒人，天网恢恢，疏而不漏，亦可畏也。

眉山主簿高公有爱子眉郎，甚慧，不幸早夭，心甚悼之。公忽暴卒，复苏，言至阴府，初为二吏来召，引至一处如州城若官府所，俄见一人着道服，手持数珠而出。主簿熟视，乃其父也。责之曰：汝有不公当事，还曾知否？主簿曰：何事不公当也？父曰：断递铺杀人事，不穷其理，以直为曲，所以天夺汝爱儿眉郎，见亦在此，汝有阴骘，天未遽夺汝寿，汝今还世，切须事君则忠，事长则顺，不可为己营私，不可以直为曲，戒杀戒淫，戒嗔戒怒，但依吾教，则尽天年，不然则寿禄皆削也。
海盐县蒋十八居士、蒋念二孺人，日诵大乘，断除嗜欲，一日洗漱更衣，烧香念佛，书颂而终。居士颂曰：这个幻身，四大合成。今日分散，各归其根。诸幻既灭，灰飞烟绝。如空中风，犹碧天月。既无障碍，又能皎洁。一切永断，无复言说。又云：直道而行，心不谄曲。四十年来，脱离嗜欲。唯阐大乘，朝读暮读。今朝摆手西归，自有现成果足。蒋孺人颂曰：看过莲经万四千，平生香火有因缘。西方自是吾归路，风月同乘般若船。

江南平建州有大将余洪敬妻郑氏有绝色，为乱兵所获，献于裨将王建峰，遇以非礼，郑志不可夺，胁以白刃不屈。又命引所掠妇人，令郑杀以食之。谓郑曰：汝惧乎？曰：此身宁早充君庖，誓不可以非礼污我。竟不忍杀，以献大将军查文徽，将以荐枕。郑大骂曰：王师吊伐，义夫节妇，宜加旌赏。王司徒出于卒伍，固无足怪，君侯知书，为国上将，乃欲加非礼于一妇人以逞欲乎？愿速见杀。查大惭，求其夫而付之。郑氏节义凛凛，二将虎狼，终不敢犯。妇人之淫奔无耻者视此独不面乎？

华亭人黄翁，世以卖香为业。后徙居东湖杨柳巷，世以卖香为生。每往临安江下收买甜头，归家修事为香货卖。甜头者，香行俚语也，乃海南贩到柏木及藤头是也。黄遂将此木断截挑棺，如笺香片子，与蕃香相和，上甑内蒸透，以米汤调合墨水，用茆帚蘸墨水就甑内翻洒，此香遍斑，取出摊干，上市货卖。淳熙年间，黄翁一日驾舟欲归华亭，留东湖湖口，泊船而宿，候晓即行。湖口有金山大王庙，灵威人皆敬畏之。是夜三鼓时，忽一人扯起黄翁，连拳殴之，谓曰：汝何作业造罪，货卖假香，可速去来。过更时许，方得苏醒。次日抵舍，病月馀而毙。一夕，其妻黄嫂梦至阴府，见二鬼以沸汤两桶洗一罪人，鬼遂叱黄嫂曰：婆子，此汝之夫黄某也，在世货卖假香，今受此报，汝今回世，说与诸子，速改此业。黄嫂寤，悲泣言及诸子，即饭僧修设功德，追救其夫，遂改业别为生理。
海盐县倪生每用杂木碎М炒磨为末，号曰印香，发贩货卖。一夜烧薰蚊虫药爆，少火入印香箩内，遂起烟焰，事急，用水浇之。傍有切香，亦见焚煅，又用水浇之。磨上印香又燃。倪见火势难遏，即欲出户逃命，奈何遍室烟迷而不能出避。须臾，人屋一火而尽。

嘉兴府周大郎每卖香时，才与人评值，或疑其不中，周曰：此香若不好，愿出门当逢恶神扑死。常以此誓为词。淳年间，忽日过府后桥，如逢一物绊倒。众即扶持，气已绝矣。呜呼！世人焚香，诚欲供养三界十方贤圣，黄翁、倪生与周大郎者，乃以废木为真，触秽神祗，岂得不遭诛戮哉！ 有人好道，不知其方。朝夕拜一枯树，辄云乞长生。如此二十八年不倦，一旦木生紫花，甘津如密，食之即仙去。 黄觉旅舍见道士共饮，举杯之际，道士以箸蘸酒于案上写吕字，觉悟其为洞宾也，遂肃然起敬。道士又于袖中出大钱七、小钱三，曰：数不可益也。又与药寸许，岁旦以酒磨服之，可终岁无疾。如其言至七十馀，药亦尽。作诗云：床头历日无多了，屈指明年七十三。于是岁卒。

陈元植好积阴德，禽鸟悉蒙其惠。每食，高原之上百鸟飞鸣就食。一夕梦绯衣人曰：汝有阴德及物，寿本不逾四十，延至九十九，无疾而终。 周世宗毁铜佛像，曰：佛教以头目髓脑有利于众生，尚无所惜，宁复以铜像为爱乎？镇州大悲铜像甚有灵应。击毁之，以斧钺自胸破。其后世宗北征，疽发胸间，咸以为报应云。 李主簿夜泊舟，临舷濯足，忽有物在水中掣其足，众力救之，李号呼曰：痛彻心骨，不可忍，吾宁死也。遂随入水，明日求其尸，不获。 晋周兴死而复生，言天帝召见，升殿仰视，云气紫郁郁然。天帝面方一尺，问左右曰：是张天帝耶？答曰：上古天帝久已圣矣，此近曹明帝耳。 李舟之弟患风，或云蛇酒治风。乃求黑蛇，生置瓮中，酝以曲ろ，数日蛇声不绝。及熟，香气酷烈，引满而饮，斯须之间化为水，惟毛发存焉。

茅山有村儿牧牛，洗所著汗衫，曝于草上，牛食草之际，并食其衫。疑邻儿窃之，其父怒曰：生儿为盗，将安用之！即将儿投于水中。邻儿称冤呼天，才出水，父复投之。俄大雷雨震死其牛，汗衫自牛口中出。 钱处士尝见一人，谓曰：尔天罚将及，可急告谢其人，曰：某平生无过，但昨日饮食不如意，弃于沟中。钱曰：是也，可急取食之。乃以水沃去其秽，俄雷电大震，钱曰：急，并秽食之。雷电果息。 惠州一娼震死于市，胁下有朱书云：李林甫以毒虐弄权，帝命震死此女，盖偃月公后身也。元和六年六月某日。 金蚕，蚕金色，食以蜀锦，取其遗粪置饮食中，毒人必死。喜能致他财，使人暴富，遣之极难，虽水火兵刃不能害。多以金银藏箧，置蚕其中，投之路隅，人或收之以去，谓之嫁金蚕。

临江军惠历寺，初造轮藏成，僧限千钱则转一匝。有营妓丧夫，家极贫，念欲转藏以资冥福。累月辛苦，求舍随缘，终不满一千。迫于贫乏，无以自存，且嫁有日矣，此心眷眷不能已，乃携所聚之钱，号泣藏前，掷钱拜地，轮藏自转，阖寺骇异。自是不复限数矣。 有赵小子纳凉水滨，见行贾掬水灌漱，俯身潭上，一鬼自潭引手至项上，三进三止。赵叫呼，鬼即随没。贾曰：头髻中有少雄黄辟邪之效也。 南阳人侯庆有一铜像，欲卖牛妆金色，偶有急事，他用久矣。一夕庆妻忽梦像曰：卿夫妻负我金色，久不偿，今取卿儿丑以偿金色。至晓儿丑有病，像忽有金色，光照四邻，皆来观焉。 零陵太守有女，悦父书吏，无计得偶。使婢取书吏所饮馀水饮之，因有娠，生一男。数岁太守莫知其所从来。一日使是男求其父，儿直入书吏幄中，化为水。父大惊，问其女，始言其故。遂以女妻之。

有人好餐羊头，尝晨出，见一羊头人身，衣冠甚伟，曰：吾未位之神也，其属在羊。尔食羊头甚多，故来取汝。若辍食则已，不然吾将杀汝。其人惧，不复食羊。 雷州西有雷公庙，百姓岁纳雷鼓车。人有以黄鱼与彘肉同食，立遭雷震。每大雷，人多于野中掘得{石}石，号雷公墨，光莹如漆。 婺源公山二洞有穴，咸通末有郑道士以绳缒下百馀丈，傍有光，往视之，路穷水阻。隔岸有花木，二道士对棋，使一童子剌船而至，问欲渡否，答曰：当还。童子回舟去。郑复攀绳而出，明日穴中有石笋塞其口，自是无复入者。 终南山中有一人，身无衣服，遍体生黑毛，飞腾不可及。为猎人所得，言秦宫人，避乱入山，有老翁教食松实。初甚苦涩，后稍便之，遂不饥。猎人以谷食之，初闻甚臭，吐逆数日乃安，身毛脱落，渐老而死。

朱师古，眉州人，年三十时得疾不能食，闻荤腥即呕。用火铛旋煮汤沃淡饭，数数食之，医莫能治。史载之曰：俗辈不读医经而妄欲疗人，可叹也。君之疾正在《素问经》中，名食挂。凡人肺六叶，舒张如盖，下覆于脾，子母气和则进食，一或有戾则肺不能舒，脾为之蔽，故不嗜食。《素问》曰：肺叶隽热挂。遂授一方，买药服之，三日闻人食肉甚香，取而啖之，遂愈。 欧阳文忠公庆历末水宿采石渡，舟人鼾睡，渐至月黑，公灭烛方寝，微闻呼声曰：去来。舟尾答曰：有参政船宿此，不可擅去，斋料幸携至。公私念曰：舟尾逼浦，且无从人，必鬼也。通夕不寐，五鼓闻岸上猎猎驰骤声，舟尾曰：斋料幸见还，且行。且答曰：道场不清净，无所得而归。公异之。后日游金山与长老瑞新语，曰：某夜有施主设水陆携室人至，方拜忽思卧，少顷乳一子，俄腥风灭烛，一众尽恐。乃公宿采石之夜也，公后果参大政。
蔡元度适余杭，舟次泗州，僧伽吐光射其舟，万人仰瞻。有按呈露士大夫知元度不起矣，至高邮而没。世言元度乃木义后身云。 有人得青石，大如砖，背有鼻，穿铁索，长数丈，循环无相断处，海商见之，以数十千易之，云此协金石。垂于海中，经夕引出，上必有金。 西域胡僧咒人能生死，太宗令壮勇者试之，如言而死，如言而苏。傅奕曰：此邪法也，邪不犯正，若咒臣必不行。召僧咒，奕初无所觉，胡僧自倒，更不复苏。

天复中陇右大饥，其年秋稼甚丰，将刈之间，太半无穗。有就田畔鼠穴求之，所获甚多，于是家家穷穴，有获五七斛者。相传谓之劫鼠仓，饥民皆出求食，济活甚众。 夷陵有阴阳石，阴石常润，阳石常燥，旱则鞭阴石，雨则鞭阳石，皆应。 韦思玄求炼金术，一日有居士辛锐来谒，病痈，溃血且甚，韦方会客，居士遂溺于筵上，客怒皆起，锐亦告去，忽不见。视其溺乃紫金液，光彩灿然，客有解者曰：辛属金，兑西方属金，锐其金精乎？ 南海小虞山有鬼母，一产千鬼，朝产之，暮食之。今苍梧有鬼姑神是也，虎头龙足，蟒目蚊眉。 荆南都头李遇病困，摄至阴府，有一先物故者曰：常侍安得来？此乡有一人云进到李遇□□，见妻子环泣身下，卧一昼，人号替代云。 王洙避暑神庙，见一老人佗背，及肋有搭，曰处，明日视之，乃橐驼也。昨夕所见岂其精耶？
